next_head_excerpt:
# Chapter 11

The first time was in his room, because her room had a window that faced the floodlit outdoor track and neither of them wanted to see it.

His diagnostic display sat on standby, a dim green pulse on the wall, the numbers reduced to a single status bar. Heart rate, bridge latency, graft integration. All normal. He'd stopped checking it every hour about a week ago. That probably counted as progress.

Lena sat at the head of the bed with her knees drawn up and her phone facedown on the mattress. She'd come over after the pool. Her hair was still damp, the chlorine smell so constant on her that he only noticed it now when she was close. They'd been talking about nothing, a movie someone had put on in the common room, whether the cafeteria coffee was getting worse or they were all just adjusting to the altitude. The conversation had run down the way conversations do when neither person wants to leave but neither has a reason to stay that they're ready to name.

He was sitting at the other end of the bed. His left wrist was on his knee, the ache doing its low-grade thing, the bone reminding him it had been broken and reassembled on a schedule the rest of his body hadn't agreed to. The scar tissue on his cheek had smoothed to a pink shine. Six weeks ago he'd been bleeding on the track with his legs still twitching. Now he was sitting on a narrow institutional bed with a woman who smelled like chlorine, and the distance between those two states had no name he could find.

"You're staring at your wrist," Lena said.

"I'm not staring."

"You're looking at it the way you...
open_hooks_to_carry: Margot experiences Lena's desync from inside the feed—personality thinning to nothing, replaced by pure system optimization
Margot pushes Lena's heart rate into the red zone during the desync
Lena surfaces unable to remember the last two laps
Margot lies when Lena asks what happened in the water

payoffs_to_land.description: The desync is far worse than anyone has reported—Lena loses entire segments of conscious experience

prev_tail_excerpt:
..."I filed a complaint. The investigation found that protocols had been followed, and they had been. Nobody wanted to look at whether the protocols should have existed in the first place." She turned the empty cup between her palms. "I'm not telling you this so you'll feel sorry for me."

"Petra."

"I'm not going to report you."

He looked at her then.

"I don't want details of your personal research," she said. "I don't want information that would oblige me to disclose more than I already know. What I am telling you is that the transfer logs will be audited. Two weeks, two months, I cannot say. But they will be audited."

"I know."

"Then decide what you are going to do before someone else decides it for you."

She stood, took her cup, and walked to the door. Her hand found the plate and stayed there.

"If this alternative approach shows clinical promise," she said, still facing the corridor, "I want to see the data before it goes anywhere near a live system. That is not a request from the medical director. It is a request from a physician."

"Understood."

"Good night, Dr. Qadir."

"Night."

The door shut. The bay settled back into machine hum and ceiling-light buzz. Yusuf pulled the patch window full-screen. Thirty-four of forty-seven handshake variants mapped. He keyed in variant thirty-five by hand because his fingers needed the work.

Outside the window the mountains had started to separate from the dark. On the display the replay hit the hook, hesitated, and spilled a new column of latency numbers down the pane. He leaned closer before he had even read the first one.
state_deltas_required: Margot's addiction exploits Lena's vulnerability at the worst possible moment
Coach-athlete trust revealed as exploitative; Lena's desync severity concealed to protect Margot's access
